front: Sculpture: equestrian bronze statue of Peter the Great, Falconet's best-known work, erected in Senate Square, St. Petersburg
Sculpture: its pedestal is the Thunder Stone, a 1,250-ton granite monolith — the largest single stone ever moved by humans
Sculpture: commissioned by Catherine the Great as a monument to Peter the Great
Sculpture: the head of Peter the Great in this work was sculpted by Falconet's student and daughter-in-law Marie-Anne Collot
Sculpture: its Thunder Stone pedestal was transported using a bronze-sphere sled devised by Marinos Carburis
Sculpture: gave its title to a famous Alexander Pushkin poem
Sculpture: depicts Peter the Great trampling a snake beneath his horse's hooves
Sculpture: Denis Diderot suggested Falconet be commissioned for this work
Sculpture: a seated Cupid holding a finger to his lips; a stone copy appears on the left side of Fragonard's The Swing
back: Cupid Sculptures: Mischievous Cupid / Menacing Love (Étienne Maurice Falconet)
work: Cupid Sculptures: Mischievous Cupid / Menacing Love

front: Sculpture: Mischievous Cupid provided the model for watchful statues that appear in Fragonard's paintings of loving couples
back: Cupid Sculptures: Mischievous Cupid / Menacing Love (Étienne Maurice Falconet)
work: Cupid Sculptures: Mischievous Cupid / Menacing Love

front: Sculpture: discussed in Mary Sheriff's Moved by Love; shows a cupid kissing the hand of a woman on a pedestal as her creator kneels before her
back: Cupid Sculptures: Mischievous Cupid / Menacing Love (Étienne Maurice Falconet)
work: Cupid Sculptures: Mischievous Cupid / Menacing Love

front: Sculpture: early work that won Falconet wide renown; Pierre Puget also sculpted this subject, showing him struggling against a lion clawing at his thigh
Artist: preeminent French Rococo sculptor of the 18th century, best known for an equestrian statue of Peter the Great
Artist: contributed 'Reflections on Sculpture' to Diderot's Encyclopédie; close friend of Denis Diderot
Artist: worked at the Sèvres Manufactory, where he created his Bather and small figurines of children based on François Boucher paintings
Artist: patronized by Madame de Pompadour during his early Paris career
